text: Following the placement of robotic ports, the surgeon performs a formal laparoscopy, identifying the mass and the distal tattoo within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealing device to take down peritoneal adhesions, carefully separating the adhesions from surrounding tissue.
Lighted ureteral stents are visible throughout the surgical field, enhanced with intraoperative fluorescent techniques to improve visualization during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric vessels and confirms the ureter's location. A white load robotic stapler is used to transect the vessels, visibly separating them.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and proceeds to take down the mesentery using a robotic vessel sealing device, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon continues dissection along the mesentery in both proximal and distal directions using robotic assistance.
The surgeon continues to take down the mesentery using a robotic vessel sealing device, progressing until grossly negative margins are visible away from the tattooed site.
The surgeon thins out the colonic mesentery and uses a green load robotic stapler to transect the distal sigmoid colon from the proximal rectum.
The specimen is extracorporealized and transected, followed by the placement of an EEA anvil. The descending colon is then returned to the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon inserts an EEA stapler through the rectal stump to perform a circular stapled anastomosis. After completing the anastomosis, the stapler is removed, and the pelvis is filled with saline. The descending colon is clamped, and a rigid sigmoidoscope is reinserted into the rectum, confirming a negative leak test.
The robotic system is docked to begin the liver portion of the hepatectomy. An intraoperative ultrasound is utilized to visually identify the liver lesion.
The surgeon uses monopolar instrumentation to take down the falciform ligament, detaching it from the surrounding liver tissue.
The surgeon holds the left lobe of the liver while dissecting the left triangular and coronary ligaments. The gastrohepatic ligament is also opened, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to identify the plane of liver transection, marking out the resection margin with monopolar instrumentation.
The surgeon opens the resection plane using monopolar instrumentation, followed by the use of a vessel sealing device to further transect the plane.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to maintain hemostasis, ensuring minimal bleeding in the resection area of the liver.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic vascular stapling device to complete the liver transection, which is more than two centimeters deep.
The surgeon divides the left hepatic vein intrahepatically, followed by further hemostasis using monopolar instrumentation to control bleeding in the transection area.
The surgeon performs additional suturing to enhance hemostasis and prevent biliary leakage, ensuring the surgical site is secure and free from bleeding.
The resected liver tissue is placed into a retrieval bag and removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon applies fibrin sealant over the transection margin of the liver to enhance hemostasis and seal the tissue.
